panels.title: CPU usage
panels.description: JVM process CPU usage (0–1 = 0–100%) and system CPU usage across all cores.

panels.title: Memory usage
panels.description: JVM heap memory used vs max, and non-heap (Metaspace, CodeCache).

panels.title: Task Dispatch Timeouts (poll_timeouts)
panels.description:
Rate of tasks queued on the server that were not picked up by a worker before the poll window expired. Broken down by task_type (Workflow / Activity) and task queue. Source: Temporal server.

Note: SDK-level execution timeouts (workflow/activity start-to-close) are not exposed as a separate counter in Java SDK 1.32 — they surface as failures in Workflow Failures By Type and Activity Failed panels.

panels.title: Service Request Rate
panels.description: Rate of gRPC requests handled by each Temporal server component (frontend, history, matching). Frontend handles client/worker calls; history manages workflow state; matching dispatches tasks to workers.

panels.title: Service Errors by Type
panels.description: Rate of server-side errors broken down by error type and service. serviceerror_NotFound on SignalWorkflowExecution = signal sent to already-completed workflow (normal). serviceerror_Unavailable or ResourceExhausted = server under pressure.

panels.title: Service Latency p95 (ms)
panels.description: p95 latency of gRPC calls handled internally by the Temporal server, per service. Elevated frontend latency directly delays workflow starts and signal delivery. Elevated history latency slows workflow task processing.

panels.title: Persistence Latency p95 (ms)
panels.description: p95 latency of database operations (Postgres) per operation type. Temporal is heavily DB-dependent: every workflow state transition writes to persistence. Latency above 10ms here will slow the entire cluster.

panels.title: Persistence Errors
panels.description: Rate of database errors by type. serviceerror_NotFound on GetTaskQueue is normal on startup. Sustained errors on CreateWorkflowExecution or UpdateWorkflowExecution indicate DB problems.

panels.title: Worker Saturation — Schedule-to-Start / Slots / Backlog
panels.description:
Three saturation signals on one panel.

Left axis: schedule-to-start p95 (ms) — time tasks wait before a worker picks them up.
Right axis: slots available (count) and task queue backlog (count).

Saturation pattern: schedule-to-start rises AND slots-available drops toward 0 AND backlog grows. All three moving together confirms the worker is overwhelmed. schedule-to-start rising alone (slots still available) usually means the Temporal server is slow — check Service Latency.
